message:
feat(embed,fsfs,index): in-place L2 normalize, symlink snapshot safety, ANN soft-delete + WAL merge

Embedding normalization (fastembed_embedder.rs):
- Replace allocating l2_normalize() with in-place normalize_in_place() for both
  single and batch embedding paths, eliminating intermediate Vec allocations
- Handle degenerate norm (NaN/subnormal) by zeroing the vector instead of
  propagating garbage values
- Remove unused l2_normalize import from traits

Filesystem watcher snapshots (watcher.rs):
- Add symlink-aware handling in collect_snapshot_for_root(): resolve symlinks
  to check if target is a file before treating as single-file root
- Skip broken symlinks gracefully (NotFound → Ok(())) instead of erroring
- Propagate is_symlink flag through DiscoveryCandidate::with_symlink()

Two-tier ANN search correctness (two_tier.rs):
- Filter soft-deleted records from HNSW ANN results (static index doesn't
  reflect deletions) by checking is_deleted() on each hit
- Fetch extra candidates (k+10) to compensate for filtered tombstones
- Merge WAL entries not yet in the ANN index: compute dot products against
  query, assign virtual indices matching VectorIndex::resolve_wal_hit logic
- Re-sort and truncate merged results to maintain top-k invariant

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-1b8","title":"MRL Adaptive Dimensionality at Search Time","description":"Implement Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL) adaptive dimensionality for search-time dimension truncation. MRL-trained models (which includes potion-128M and many modern sentence transformers) produce embeddings where the first N dimensions carry the most information. We can search with truncated embeddings for speed, then use full dimensions for re-scoring.

## Design

```rust
pub struct MrlConfig {
    pub search_dims: usize,     // Dimensions for initial scan (default: 64)
    pub rescore_dims: usize,    // Dimensions for re-scoring top candidates (default: full)
    pub rescore_top_k: usize,   // Re-score this many candidates (default: 3x limit)
    pub enabled: bool,          // Default: false
}

impl VectorIndex {
    /// Search using only first `search_dims` dimensions, then re-score top candidates
    /// with full `rescore_dims` dimensions for better accuracy.
    pub fn mrl_search(
        &self,
        query: &[f32],
        limit: usize,
        config: &MrlConfig,
    ) -> Vec<VectorHit>;
}
```

## Algorithm

1. Truncate query to first search_dims dimensions
2. Scan all vectors using only first search_dims dimensions (fewer multiply-accumulate ops)
3. Collect top rescore_top_k candidates
4. Re-score candidates using full rescore_dims dimensions
5. Return top limit results

## Performance Model

- Standard search (384 dims): 384 multiply-accumulate per vector
- MRL search (64 + rescore 30): 64*N + 384*30 = 64N + 11520
- Break-even at N ≈ 36 vectors. For N > 100, MRL is faster.
- For 10K vectors: 640K vs 3.84M ops = 6x speedup on initial scan
- Net speedup depends on rescore_top_k but typically 2-4x for large indices

## SIMD Considerations

- 64 dims = 8 f32x8 operations (perfect alignment)
- 128 dims = 16 f32x8 operations
- Truncation points should be multiples of 8 for SIMD efficiency

## Index Format

No FSVI changes needed. We store full-dimension vectors and truncate at search time. The dimension information is in the header, and truncation is a runtime operation.

## Why This Matters

For large indices (100K+ vectors), search latency is dominated by the dot product scan. MRL gives a 2-4x speedup with minimal quality loss because the first 64 dimensions of MRL-trained models capture 90%+ of the variance. This is particularly valuable for the fast tier where latency budget is tight (<15ms).

## Testing

- Unit: truncated search returns same top-1 as full search (on MRL-trained embeddings)
- Unit: search_dims must be <= actual dimension
- Unit: SIMD alignment (search_dims multiple of 8)
- Unit: rescore_top_k < limit → fall back to full search
- Integration: MRL search quality vs full search (recall@10 comparison)
- Benchmark: speedup factor for various index sizes and search_dims","acceptance_criteria":"1. The feature is fully implemented according to this bead design, scope, and referenced files or modules.
2. Integration with dependent components is complete and behavior remains correct across normal and degraded flows.
3. Edge and error cases are explicitly handled with deterministic outcomes and clear reason codes or errors.
4. Unit and integration tests (and e2e where relevant) are added, passing, and include detailed structured logging assertions.
5. Performance and resource expectations in the bead are validated and documented with reproducible evidence.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T22:00:36.745371880Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-14T04:09:37.198267671Z","closed_at":"2026-02-14T04:09:37.198245470Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1b8","depends_on_id":"bd-3un","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T22:12:48.647756312Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1b8","depends_on_id":"bd-3un.13","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T22:02:27.829647768Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1b8","depends_on_id":"bd-3un.14","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T22:02:27.940407775Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1b8","depends_on_id":"bd-3un.15","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T22:02:28.046355974Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":309,"issue_id":"bd-1b8","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"REFINEMENT PASS 1: No asupersync needed -- MRL search is a synchronous computation (truncated dot product scan + full-dimension rescore). The SIMD alignment note (search_dims should be multiples of 8 for f32x8) is critical and well-specified. BODY QUALITY: The performance model is excellent with concrete break-even analysis. INTERACTION with bd-1hw (incremental FSVI): MRL search must also work with WAL records. Since MRL operates on stored full-dimension vectors and truncates at search time, this should work transparently -- WAL records store the same full-dimension vectors.","created_at":"2026-02-13T22:07:39Z"},{"id":337,"issue_id":"bd-1b8","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"REFINEMENT PASS 4 (testing gaps): Missing edge-case tests:
- [ ] Unit: search_dims == actual dimension (no truncation, MRL is a no-op -- should produce identical results to standard search)
- [ ] Unit: search_dims > actual dimension -- should return error or clamp to actual dimension
- [ ] Unit: search_dims = 0 -- degenerate case, should error
- [ ] Unit: empty index (0 vectors) -- mrl_search returns empty vec
- [ ] Unit: rescore_top_k = 0 -- should error or return empty
- [ ] Unit: single vector in index -- trivially returns it regardless of dims
- [ ] Unit: verify truncated dot product uses ONLY first search_dims elements (no out-of-bounds)
- [ ] Integration: MRL search with WAL records (bd-1hw interaction) -- truncated search sees WAL vectors
All existing 6 tests are well-specified. These edge cases protect against off-by-one and degenerate input bugs.
","created_at":"2026-02-13T22:18:34Z"},{"id":391,"issue_id":"bd-1b8","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"CRATE PLACEMENT: frankensearch-index (MRL search is an optimization of the vector search scan. Lives alongside search.rs, potentially as an extension method on VectorIndex. New file: index/src/mrl.rs)","created_at":"2026-02-13T22:50:55Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1co","title":"Index Warm-Up and Adaptive Prefault Strategy","description":"Implement index warm-up and adaptive page prefaulting for memory-mapped FSVI indices. Cold-start latency for mmap'd indices can be 10-100x higher than warm due to page faults. This bead adds controlled prefaulting to eliminate cold-start variance.

## Background

Frankensearch's FSVI indices are memory-mapped for zero-copy access. This is optimal for steady-state performance: the OS page cache keeps frequently-accessed pages in memory, and search reads directly from mapped memory without any copying. However, the first search after process start (or after memory pressure evicts pages) triggers a cascade of page faults that makes cold-start search dramatically slower.

For xf with 50K documents (~20MB index), cold search is approximately 50ms vs ~1ms warm — a 50x difference. For larger indices, this gap grows. This latency variance is problematic for interactive applications where consistent sub-10ms response times are expected.

## Design

### Configuration

```rust
pub struct WarmUpConfig {
    pub strategy: WarmUpStrategy,
    pub max_bytes: usize,        // Budget: don't prefault more than this (default: 256MB)
    pub parallel_readers: usize, // Concurrent prefault threads (default: 2)
}

pub enum WarmUpStrategy {
    None,                        // No prefaulting (current behavior)
    Full,                        // Touch every page sequentially
    Header,                      // Prefault header + record table only (smallest footprint)
    Adaptive(AdaptiveConfig),    // Heat-map based intelligent prefaulting
}

pub struct AdaptiveConfig {
    pub heat_decay: f64,         // Exponential decay factor (default: 0.95)
    pub min_heat: f64,           // Minimum heat to prefault (default: 0.1)
}
```

### Adaptive Strategy

The adaptive strategy learns which pages are actually accessed during typical queries:

1. Maintain a per-page heat map: 1 bit per 4KB page = 32KB overhead for a 1GB index
2. Increment heat on every page access (detected via access pattern tracking at the VectorIndex level)
3. Heat decays exponentially per search cycle: heat *= heat_decay
4. On warm-up, prefault pages above min_heat threshold in heat-descending order (hottest pages first)
5. Stop when max_bytes budget is exhausted

This means after a few search cycles, the system learns that (for example) the header, record table, and the first 30% of vector slabs are "hot" and should be prefaulted, while the remaining 70% of vectors (rarely-accessed tail) can be faulted on demand.

### OS Integration

- Linux: `madvise(MADV_WILLNEED)` to request kernel prefaulting
- macOS: `madvise(MADV_WILLNEED)` (same API, different kernel behavior)
- Fallback: sequential read through targeted pages for portability on other platforms

## Justification

In production, frankensearch indices are memory-mapped for zero-copy access. But the first search after process start (or after memory pressure evicts pages) is dramatically slower due to page faults. Prefaulting eliminates this variance.

The adaptive strategy is key: rather than always prefaulting the entire index (wasteful for large indices where only a fraction of pages are typically accessed), we learn which pages are actually accessed during typical queries and only prefault those. This provides the benefits of full prefaulting (consistent latency) with a fraction of the memory and I/O cost.

## Considerations

- Memory pressure: prefaulting competes with other processes for page cache space. The max_bytes budget prevents frankensearch from evicting other processes' pages.
- Background prefaulting: warm-up should run in background threads so it doesn't block the first search. If a search arrives before warm-up completes, it proceeds normally (with possible page faults for non-prefaulted pages).
- Multiple indices: when multiple FSVI indices are open (e.g., fast + quality embeddings), warm-up should respect the total max_bytes budget across all indices.
- Interaction with S3-FIFO cache (bd-l7v): the cache and prefaulting are complementary — cache handles repeated access patterns, prefaulting handles cold start.

## Testing

- [ ] Unit: WarmUpStrategy::None is a no-op (no system calls)
- [ ] Unit: WarmUpStrategy::Header only touches header + record table bytes
- [ ] Unit: heat map increment/decay math is correct
- [ ] Unit: max_bytes budget is respected (never prefault more than budget)
- [ ] Unit: heat map size calculation for various index sizes
- [ ] Integration: verify cold-start latency improvement (before/after warm-up, measure p50/p99)
- [ ] Integration: verify warm-up completes within reasonable time (< 2s for 256MB budget)
- [ ] Benchmark: prefault overhead vs cold search latency savings (quantify the tradeoff)","acceptance_criteria":"1. The feature is fully implemented according to this bead design, scope, and referenced files or modules.
2. Integration with dependent components is complete and behavior remains correct across normal and degraded flows.
3. Edge and error cases are explicitly handled with deterministic outcomes and clear reason codes or errors.
4. Unit and integration tests (and e2e where relevant) are added, passing, and include detailed structured logging assertions.
5. Performance and resource expectations in the bead are validated and documented with reproducible evidence.
6. Every explicit checklist item in the bead description is implemented and verified by automated tests or validation commands.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T22:00:30.949232738Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-14T06:02:25.345643303Z","closed_at":"2026-02-14T03:46:23.316415319Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1co","depends_on_id":"bd-3un","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-14T01:21:07.882805121Z","created_by":"PinkCanyon","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1co","depends_on_id":"bd-3un.13","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T22:02:20.353734149Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":307,"issue_id":"bd-1co","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"REFINEMENT PASS 1: Background prefaulting uses std::thread or Rayon, NOT asupersync. The madvise(MADV_WILLNEED) call is a synchronous syscall. The parallel_readers config spawns OS threads for prefaulting, which is correct since this is IO-bound work that benefits from OS thread scheduling. The heat map uses atomic operations (AtomicU8 per page) for lock-free concurrent updates during search. No Cx needed. PRIORITY NOTE: P3 seems appropriate -- this is an optimization, not a correctness requirement. Cold-start latency is a real issue but only affects the first query.","created_at":"2026-02-13T22:07:27Z"},{"id":319,"issue_id":"bd-1co","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"REFINEMENT PASS 1: DEPENDENCY CONCERN - This bead depends on bd-l7v (S3-FIFO cache). The body mentions 'Interaction with S3-FIFO cache (bd-l7v): the cache and prefaulting are complementary.' But is this a hard dependency? Warm-up/prefaulting can be implemented without the cache existing. The cache is a separate layer. Consider whether this dep should be removed to avoid blocking warm-up on the cache implementation. The two features are complementary but independent.","created_at":"2026-02-13T22:09:24Z"},{"id":320,"issue_id":"bd-1co","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"REFINEMENT PASS 1: Removed hard dep on bd-l7v (S3-FIFO cache). Warm-up/prefaulting is completely independent of the cache layer. They are complementary (cache handles repeated access, prefaulting handles cold start) but neither blocks the other. The interaction note in the body is sufficient -- no formal dependency needed.","created_at":"2026-02-13T22:09:40Z"},{"id":334,"issue_id":"bd-1co","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"REFINEMENT PASS 4 (testing gaps): Missing edge-case tests:
- Unit: empty index (0 vectors, 0 pages) -- warm-up should be a no-op
- Unit: adaptive heat map with no prior access history (first warm-up after fresh start) -- should fall back to Header strategy or prefault nothing
- Unit: concurrent warm-up + search safety -- warm-up thread and search thread accessing mmap simultaneously (madvise is safe but test should verify no panics)
- Unit: index smaller than one page (< 4KB) -- edge case for heat map size calculation
- Integration: warm-up interrupted (thread cancelled mid-prefault) -- verify index is still in consistent state
All existing tests are well-specified. These additions cover edge cases that could cause subtle bugs in production.
","created_at":"2026-02-13T22:18:17Z"},{"id":393,"issue_id":"bd-1co","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"CRATE PLACEMENT: frankensearch-index (warm-up and prefaulting operate on memory-mapped FSVI files. New file: index/src/warmup.rs)","created_at":"2026-02-13T22:51:00Z"},{"id":398,"issue_id":"bd-1co","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"DESIGN CLARIFICATION: Safe madvise usage under #![forbid(unsafe_code)].

The project forbids unsafe code (#![forbid(unsafe_code)] per AGENTS.md). The body mentions madvise(MADV_WILLNEED) which normally requires unsafe FFI. However, the memmap2 crate (already a dependency) provides a SAFE wrapper:

  use memmap2::Advice;
  mmap.advise(Advice::WillNeed)?;  // Safe! No unsafe block needed.

This is the correct approach for frankensearch. The memmap2::Mmap::advise() method handles the FFI boundary safely. We do NOT need raw libc::madvise calls.

The fallback path (sequential read for portability) uses std::fs::File::read() which is also safe.

NO unsafe code is needed for this bead.","created_at":"2026-02-13T22:51:47Z"},{"id":750,"issue_id":"bd-1co","author":"PinkCanyon","text":"[bd-17dv retrofit] DEP_SEMANTICS: PARENT_CHILD bd-3un (program grouping only). HARD_DEP bd-3un.13 (FSVI mmap layout required for prefault strategy). SOFT_DEP bd-l7v cache interaction remains documented but intentionally not modeled as a blocker edge (independent optimizations).","created_at":"2026-02-14T01:21:39Z"},{"id":767,"issue_id":"bd-1co","author":"PinkCanyon","text":"[bd-2l7y baseline-budget] BASELINE_COMPARATOR: no prefault/warm-up (pure demand paging cold-start behavior). BUDGETED_MODE_DEFAULTS: warmup_strategy=adaptive, max_prefault_bytes=268435456, parallel_readers=2, warmup_time_budget_ms=2000, retry_budget=0. ON_EXHAUSTION: terminate warm-up early and continue with on-demand paging (no blocking). SUCCESS_THRESHOLDS_AND_STOP_CONDITIONS: cold-start p95 latency reduction >= 50% with RSS increase <= 10%; stop if prefault cost exceeds time/byte budget.","created_at":"2026-02-14T03:06:46Z"},{"id":796,"issue_id":"bd-1co","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"PERFORMANCE PROOF-LANE PROTOCOL (bd-bobf gate)

This bead is subject to the bd-bobf performance proof-lane gate. Before any performance-critical change in this bead's implementation may be considered release-ready, the following evidence artifacts must be produced and verified:

1. BASELINE BENCHMARK: Run criterion benchmarks (bd-3un.33) capturing p50/p95/p99 latency, throughput (ops/sec), and peak memory (RSS delta) for the hot path this bead optimizes. Record results in data/perf-evidence/<bead-id>-baseline.json.

2. PROFILE HOTSPOT EVIDENCE: Profile the hot path using cargo flamegraph or perf. Identify top-5 hotspot functions by cumulative time. Record in data/perf-evidence/<bead-id>-hotspots.txt.

3. OPPORTUNITY SCORE: Compute opportunity_score = (baseline_p95 / optimized_p95). Must be >= 2.0 to justify the optimization complexity. If < 2.0, document why the optimization is still warranted (e.g., tail latency improvement, memory reduction).

4. ISOMORPHISM PROOF NOTE: Document that the optimization preserves result ordering, tie-breaking determinism, floating-point reproducibility, and RNG seed behavior. Specifically: "Given identical inputs and configuration, the optimized path produces bit-identical output rankings as the unoptimized path." If not bit-identical (e.g., FP reordering), document the acceptable divergence bound.

5. GOLDEN OUTPUT VERIFICATION: Run the fixture corpus queries (bd-3un.38) through both the baseline and optimized paths. Assert identical result sets (or document bounded divergence). Include rollback command: `git revert <commit>` or feature flag to disable.

STATUS: Evidence collection is deferred until the implementation is merged and benchmarks can be run against real workloads. This comment establishes the required evidence format for release-gate compliance (bd-ehuk).
","created_at":"2026-02-14T06:02:25Z"}]}

 {"id":"bd-1zlh","title":"Fix fsfs watcher rename-event mapping to avoid stale/misindexed paths","description":"Root cause: notify rename events (Modify(Name(...))) in crates/frankensearch-fsfs/src/watcher.rs are currently normalized as generic Modified events. For rename from old->new, this loses delete/create semantics and can trigger incorrect upsert behavior for old paths. Implement explicit rename handling (old path -> Delete, new path -> Created/Upsert) and add tests for RenameMode::Both/From/To edge cases.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"NavyGull","created_at":"2026-02-14T21:10:11.940277198Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-14T21:14:13.105216972Z","closed_at":"2026-02-14T21:14:03.979541117Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":1419,"issue_id":"bd-1zlh","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Fixed: watcher rename-event mapping

**Bug**: `RenameMode::Both` used `paths.last()` to find the destination path, which
would skip the actual "to" path if more than 2 entries were present in the event.

**Fix**: Changed to `paths.get(1)` to reliably pick the second element as the
destination path regardless of vec length.

**Tests added** (4 new, 8 total rename tests):
- `rename_both_single_path_emits_only_delete`: edge case where `Both` has only 1 path
- `rename_any_existing_file_maps_to_created`: `Any` mode resolves via `symlink_metadata`
- `rename_any_missing_file_maps_to_deleted`: `Any` mode for gone files
- `rename_events_survive_debounce_independently`: verify delete+create for different paths don't coalesce

Also fixed: added missing `normalize_file_key` import in test module.

All 789 lib tests pass.
","created_at":"2026-02-14T21:14:13Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1zne","title":"Add replay classifier edge cases and enum coverage tests for catalog module","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"LavenderIbis","created_at":"2026-02-15T01:00:11.063602Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-15T01:00:15.397707208Z","closed_at":"2026-02-15T01:00:15.397685638Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fsfs","test"]}
 {"id":"bd-1zxn","title":"Program: Sprint 1 keystone unlock for core DAG compression","description":"Objective: execute a focused unlock sprint on the minimal core set that maximizes downstream parallelism.

Scope:
- Complete and verify keystone path: bd-3un.1, bd-3un.2, bd-3un.3, bd-3un.5, bd-3un.13, bd-3un.24, bd-3un.30.
- Enforce strict dependency sequencing and avoid parallel work on downstream beads until these are landed.
- Capture unblock metrics before/after (actionable count, blocked ratio, top what-if deltas).

Execution rules:
- One-lever focus: unblock-first, avoid feature sprawl.
- Every completed keystone updates triage metrics and dependency health notes.
- No new advanced feature starts before sprint gate closure unless explicitly justified in bead comments.

Deliverables:
- Closed keystone set with no unresolved blockers.
- Recorded unblock deltas and updated critical path map.
- Confirmed increase in actionable work.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Keystone path items (bd-3un.1, bd-3un.2, bd-3un.3, bd-3un.5, bd-3un.13, bd-3un.24, bd-3un.30) are explicitly tracked to closure criteria with no hidden blockers.
2. Before/after unblock metrics are captured (actionable count, blocked ratio, critical-path depth, and top what-if deltas).
3. Sprint execution log records ordering decisions, dependency-health observations, and risk calls for each keystone completion.
4. Validation includes regression smoke coverage (unit + integration + e2e entry checks) for newly unblocked surfaces.

narration.","created_at":"2026-02-13T23:27:58Z"},{"id":781,"issue_id":"bd-1zxn","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Closure evidence for bd-1zxn (keystone unlock sprint).

Keystone path verification (all closed):

- Open issues: 198 -> 196 (delta -2)
- Actionable issues: 44 -> 45 (delta +1)
- Blocked issues: 154 -> 151 (delta -3)
- Blocked ratio: 0.7778 -> 0.7704 (delta -0.00737)
- Critical-path top length: 29 -> 29 (no depth change this step)

What-if / parallelization observations:
- Before closure, `bd-1zxn` appeared in parallel-cut recommendations with `parallel_gain=1`, enabling tracks `bd-2yu.4` and `bd-2yu.6`.
- After closure, `bd-1zxn` drops from the parallel-cut set and is replaced by `bd-2yu.6.1` in top-5.
- Top what-if head shifted post-close (`bd-2hz.2` moved to rank-1 in current top_what_ifs).

Dependency-health outcomes:
- Direct dependents unblocked by this closure are now open with blocker edge satisfied:
  - bd-2yu.4 (depends on bd-1zxn + bd-2yu.2.1; both closed)
  - bd-2yu.6 (depends on bd-1zxn + bd-2yu.1.2; both closed)

Ordering decisions and risk calls:
- Decision: close this unlock bead now because all keystone dependencies were already closed and verified.
- Risk call: avoid feature sprawl here; this bead stays strictly as a DAG-compression and triage evidence gate.
- Residual risk: global critical path depth did not shrink in this single step; further compression requires closing next high-betweenness blockers (ehuk / 2hz.12 / 2hz.8.1 cluster).

Regression smoke coverage executed:
- Unit entry check: `cargo test -p frankensearch-core --lib --no-run` PASS
- Integration entry check: `cargo test -p frankensearch --test integration --no-run` PASS
- Cross-component integration check: `cargo test -p frankensearch --test cross_component --no-run` PASS
- E2E-entry style check (public entrypoints compile): `cargo check -p frankensearch --examples` PASS

Follow-on prioritized queue (post-close, by current quick wins):
1) bd-ehuk
2) bd-3w1.13
3) bd-2hz.12
4) bd-2hz.8.1
5) bd-2hz.8.2

+{"id":"bd-1zyz","title":"hnsw vectors_close returns true for Infinity vs finite values","description":"In hnsw.rs vector_component_close, when left=Inf and right=100.0: diff=Inf, scale=Inf, so Inf <= (EPSILON*8*Inf) = Inf <= Inf = true. This allows matches_vector_index to incorrectly report matching. Vectors should be normalized so Inf shouldn't appear, but the guard is missing. Fix: add is_finite check on both values.","status":"in_progress","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-02-16T04:42:20.620235655Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-16T04:42:26.236135593Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

Between drop and reopen, a concurrent writer could write valid data that gets wiped. Fix: use file locking or atomic rename.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-02-16T04:42:20.419375365Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-16T04:42:20.419375365Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-3un","title":"Epic: Create frankensearch standalone crate","description":"Extract the 2-tier hybrid search system from cass, xf, and mcp_agent_mail_rust into a standalone, reusable Rust crate called frankensearch. This crate should be drop-in for any future Rust project needing high-quality local semantic+lexical hybrid search with progressive refinement.","acceptance_criteria":"1. All child beads in this epic are implemented with dependency order respected and no unresolved blockers.
2. End-to-end behavior across the epic scope is validated via integration and e2e tests on representative fixtures.
3. Performance and quality goals described in this epic are measured and meet documented thresholds or have explicit approved exceptions.
4. Structured logging and metrics are present for all critical flows to support debugging and operational visibility.
5. Operator and developer documentation plus rollout guidance are complete, self-contained, and sufficient for future maintenance.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:46:43.968487926Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-14T02:15:14.132734507Z","closed_at":"2026-02-14T02:15:14.132666920Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["epic","meta"],"comments":[{"id":1,"issue_id":"bd-3un","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"PROJECT VISION: frankensearch is a standalone, reusable Rust crate that extracts the 2-tier hybrid search system currently duplicated across 3 projects (cass, xf, mcp_agent_mail_rust). The name 'frankensearch' reflects its Frankenstein-like assembly from the best parts of each codebase.

KEY INSIGHT (from the X post): The user wanted the best of both worlds — sub-millisecond response time (potion-128M) AND high-quality semantic understanding (MiniLM-L6-v2). Rather than choosing one, the solution is a 2-tier progressive system: show fast results immediately, then upgrade them in the background when the quality model finishes. This creates a smooth UX where results appear instantly and improve within ~150ms.

BAKEOFF RESULTS (motivation for model choices):
- FNV-1a hash: 0.07ms, no semantic meaning (fallback only)
- potion-multilingual-128M: 0.57ms, decent semantics (223x faster than MiniLM)
- all-MiniLM-L6-v2: 128ms, excellent semantics (baseline quality)
- The 223x speed gap between potion and MiniLM is exactly why the 2-tier design exists.

HYBRID SEARCH: Combines lexical (Tantivy BM25) with semantic (vector cosine similarity) via Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF, K=60). Documents appearing in both lexical AND semantic results get boosted scores, which empirically produces better results than either alone.

GOAL: Drop-in crate for any Rust project needing local search. Feature-gated so consumers pay only for what they use.","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:56:21Z"},{"id":11,"issue_id":"bd-3un","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"TESTING POLICY: Every component bead (bd-3un.6 through bd-3un.28) MUST include inline #[cfg(test)] unit tests alongside the implementation. These inline tests should cover: (a) happy path, (b) edge cases (empty input, max values, boundary conditions), (c) error conditions. The dedicated testing beads (bd-3un.31, bd-3un.32, bd-3un.40) are for CROSS-COMPONENT tests, integration tests, and e2e validation scripts -- not for basic per-component unit testing.

LOGGING POLICY: All public functions should use the tracing crate for structured logging:
- ERROR: unrecoverable failures (model load failed, index corrupted)
- WARN: degraded operation (quality model unavailable, fallback to hash)
- INFO: significant lifecycle events (index opened, model loaded, search completed)
- DEBUG: operational details (query embedding latency, candidate counts, blend scores)
- TRACE: hot-path internals (individual dot products, per-record scores) -- gated behind cfg

Every search operation should emit a tracing span with: query length, k, phase, result count, latency_ms, embedder used.
","created_at":"2026-02-13T20:11:19Z"},{"id":80,"issue_id":"bd-3un","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"CROSS-REFERENCE: FrankenSQLite Integration Epic (bd-3w1)

The FrankenSQLite integration (bd-3w1) is a sibling epic that adds:
- Persistent document storage via FrankenSQLite (replaces in-memory state)
- Self-healing indices via pervasive RaptorQ erasure coding
- FTS5 as alternative lexical engine (alongside Tantivy)

Key cross-epic dependencies:
- bd-3w1.1 (storage crate) depends on bd-3un.1 (scaffold) and bd-3un.2 (errors)
- bd-3w1.5 (durability crate) depends on bd-3un.1 (scaffold) and bd-3un.2 (errors)
- bd-3w1.7 (FSVI RaptorQ) depends on bd-3un.13 (FSVI format)
- bd-3w1.8 (Tantivy RaptorQ) depends on bd-3un.17 (Tantivy schema)
- bd-3w1.10 (FTS5) depends on bd-3un.18 (Tantivy queries, for LexicalIndex trait)
- bd-3w1.13 (pipeline) depends on bd-3un.27 (embedding job runner)
- bd-3w1.12 (staleness) depends on bd-3un.41 (staleness detection)
- bd-3w1.14 (features) depends on bd-3un.29 (feature flags)
- bd-3w1.21 (facade) depends on bd-3un.30 (public API)

The two epics can be worked in parallel: bd-3un tasks build the core search engine,
bd-3w1 tasks add persistence and durability on top.
","created_at":"2026-02-13T20:47:52Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-3un.1","title":"Scaffold Cargo workspace and crate structure","description":"Create the frankensearch Cargo workspace with the following structure:

frankensearch/
├── Cargo.toml (workspace root)
├── crates/
│   ├── frankensearch-core/      # Traits, types, error types, scoring primitives
│   │   ├── Cargo.toml
│   │   └── src/lib.rs
│   ├── frankensearch-embed/     # All embedder implementations (hash, model2vec, fastembed)
│   │   ├── Cargo.toml
│   │   └── src/lib.rs
│   ├── frankensearch-index/     # Vector index, SIMD, ANN
│   │   ├── Cargo.toml
│   │   └── src/lib.rs
│   ├── frankensearch-lexical/   # Tantivy integration (feature-gated)
│   │   ├── Cargo.toml
│   │   └── src/lib.rs
│   ├── frankensearch-fusion/    # RRF, blending, two-tier orchestration
│   │   ├── Cargo.toml
│   │   └── src/lib.rs
│   └── frankensearch-rerank/    # Reranker trait + implementations
│       ├── Cargo.toml
│       └── src/lib.rs
├── frankensearch/               # Facade crate re-exporting everything
│   ├── Cargo.toml
│   └── src/lib.rs
├── tests/
├── benches/
└── examples/

Design decisions:
- Workspace-level dep management (workspace = true pattern)
- Feature flags: 'full' (everything), 'semantic' (embedders), 'lexical' (tantivy), 'hybrid' (both), 'rerank' (rerankers)
- Rust edition 2024 (nightly), matching existing projects
- Release profile: opt-level=3, lto=true, codegen-units=1, strip=true
  NOTE: opt-level=3 (speed) NOT opt-level='z' (size). frankensearch is a performance-critical search library — SIMD dot products, embedding inference, and index traversal all benefit from aggressive optimization. Binary size is secondary to throughput.
- unsafe code: forbidden (#![forbid(unsafe_code)])

Key workspace deps (from cross-referencing cass/xf/agent-mail):
- half = '2.4' (f16 quantization)
- wide = '0.7' (SIMD f32x8)
- fastembed = '4.9' (ONNX embeddings)
- tantivy = '0.22' (full-text search)
- tokenizers = '0.21' (HuggingFace BPE)
- safetensors = '0.5' (Model2Vec weights)
- ort = '2.0.0-rc.9' (ONNX Runtime)
- asupersync (structured async runtime — mandatory, NO tokio)","acceptance_criteria":"1. The specified contract, design, or scaffold deliverable is complete, unambiguous, and aligned with existing workspace architecture.
2. Interfaces, types, and configuration semantics are explicit enough for downstream implementation without hidden assumptions.
3. Validation, error semantics, and invariants are documented and covered by conformance or unit tests.
4. Integration points with dependent beads are mapped and verified through examples or compile-time checks.
5. Required diagnostics, logging fields, and observability hooks are specified and validated.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"keystone-lane","owner":"keystone@frankensearch.local","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:47:01.537714547Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-14T00:34:13.408561969Z","closed_at":"2026-02-14T00:34:13.408535069Z","close_reason":"Scaffold complete: workspace Cargo.toml, all 6 sub-crate Cargo.toml files, facade crate, stub lib.rs files, rust-toolchain.toml, tests/benches/examples dirs. Compiles clean.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["foundation","phase1","setup"],"comments":[{"id":4,"issue_id":"bd-3un.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"CRATE STRUCTURE RATIONALE: The workspace is split into multiple sub-crates to enable:
1. Selective compilation: consumers only compile what they need
2. Parallel compilation: independent crates build concurrently
3. Clear boundaries: traits in -core, impls in domain crates
4. Testing isolation: each crate testable independently

The facade crate (frankensearch/) re-exports everything so consumers can just 'use frankensearch::*' in simple cases, or import sub-crates directly for finer control.

The 6 sub-crates map to the logical architecture:
- core: zero-dep traits/types (everything depends on this)
- embed: all embedder implementations (feature-gated per model)
- index: vector storage and search (SIMD, mmap)
- lexical: Tantivy full-text search
- fusion: RRF, blending, two-tier orchestration
- rerank: cross-encoder reranking","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:56:48Z"},{"id":153,"issue_id":"bd-3un.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"ASUPERSYNC MIGRATION — WORKSPACE DEPENDENCY UPDATE:

Add asupersync as a workspace-level dependency. Update feature flags accordingly.

BEFORE (Cargo.toml workspace deps):
  rayon = "1.10"
  crossbeam-channel = "0.5"
  reqwest = { version = "0.12", features = ["rustls-tls"], optional = true }

AFTER:
  rayon = "1.10"                    # RETAINED for data parallelism (bd-3un.15)
  asupersync = { path = "/dp/asupersync", features = ["proc-macros"] }
  # crossbeam-channel REMOVED (replaced by asupersync::channel)
  # reqwest REMOVED (replaced by asupersync::http + asupersync::tls)

FEATURE FLAGS for asupersync in frankensearch:
  - "proc-macros": scope!, spawn!, join! macros (quality of life)
  - "tls": only needed when 'download' feature is enabled
  - "sqlite": only if bd-3w1 (FrankenSQLite) integration uses asupersync's sqlite bridge
  - "metrics": optional, for OpenTelemetry integration
  - "test-internals": for LabRuntime in tests

PER-CRATE DEPENDENCIES:
  - frankensearch-core: asupersync (for sync primitives, Cx, Outcome, Error)
  - frankensearch-embed: asupersync (for Mutex, Pool, Cx)
  - frankensearch-index: rayon (data parallelism), asupersync (Cx for cancel checkpoints)
  - frankensearch-lexical: asupersync (Cx for cancel-aware Tantivy queries)
  - frankensearch-fusion: asupersync (Cx, region, scope, join, timeout, channels)
  - frankensearch-rerank: asupersync (Mutex for ONNX session)
  - frankensearch (facade): re-exports asupersync::Cx, asupersync::Outcome

ZERO-TOKIO GUARANTEE:
  After this migration, the dependency tree contains ZERO references to tokio, hyper, or any tokio-ecosystem crate. The only external async runtime is asupersync. Rayon is retained for data parallelism (not an async runtime).","created_at":"2026-02-13T21:06:20Z"},{"id":191,"issue_id":"bd-3un.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"REVISION (review pass 4 - crate structure update for trait extraction):

1. NEW SUB-CRATE: frankensearch-core must define the LexicalIndex trait (not frankensearch-lexical). This enables both Tantivy (in frankensearch-lexical) and FTS5 (in frankensearch-storage) to implement the same trait without depending on each other. The trait and its associated types go in:
   frankensearch-core/src/traits/lexical.rs

   pub trait LexicalIndex: Send + Sync {
       async fn search(&self, cx: &Cx, query: &str, limit: usize, offset: usize) -> SearchResult<Vec<LexicalHit>>;
       async fn index_document(&self, cx: &Cx, doc: &IndexableDocument) -> SearchResult<()>;
       async fn index_batch(&self, cx: &Cx, docs: &[IndexableDocument]) -> SearchResult<usize>;
       async fn delete_document(&self, cx: &Cx, doc_id: &str) -> SearchResult<bool>;
       fn document_count(&self) -> SearchResult<usize>;
       async fn optimize(&self, cx: &Cx) -> SearchResult<()>;
   }

2. UPDATED CRATE MAP (add to scaffold):
   frankensearch-core/src/
   ├── lib.rs
   ├── error.rs           (bd-3un.2)
   ├── types.rs            (bd-3un.5)
   ├── canonicalize.rs     (bd-3un.42)
   └── traits/
       ├── mod.rs
       ├── embedder.rs     (bd-3un.3: Embedder trait)
       ├── reranker.rs     (bd-3un.4: Reranker trait)
       └── lexical.rs      (LexicalIndex trait, shared by Tantivy + FTS5)

3. frankensearch-storage and frankensearch-durability sub-crates (from bd-3w1.1, bd-3w1.5) should also be listed in the workspace scaffold, even though they're feature-gated.
","created_at":"2026-02-13T21:10:27Z"},{"id":221,"issue_id":"bd-3un.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"REVIEW FIX — opt-level and workspace dependency corrections:

1. RELEASE PROFILE: The body specifies opt-level='z' (optimize for binary size). This CONFLICTS with the project's performance goals — frankensearch is a search library where SIMD throughput (bd-3un.14), vector search latency (bd-3un.15), and embedding speed are critical. Fix:

   [profile.release]
   opt-level = 3       # Maximum runtime performance (NOT 'z' which optimizes for size)
   lto = true           # Link-time optimization for cross-crate inlining
   codegen-units = 1    # Single codegen unit for better optimization opportunities
   strip = true         # Remove debug symbols from release binary

   This matches what AGENTS.md already specifies. The bead body should be updated to match.

2. MISSING WORKSPACE DEPENDENCY: Add `dirs` crate (used by bd-3un.9 for platform-specific model data directories):
   dirs = "6"    # Platform-standard data directories for model storage

3. ASUPERSYNC BEFORE/AFTER CLARIFICATION: The ASUPERSYNC comment references removing `crossbeam-channel` and `reqwest` in its BEFORE/AFTER diff, but neither appears in the original body's dependency list. Clarification: these were implicit dependencies that would have been added during implementation of bd-3un.27 (crossbeam) and bd-3un.11 (reqwest). The ASUPERSYNC comment correctly prevents them from ever being added. No action needed beyond this clarification note.

4. WORKSPACE-LEVEL DEV-DEPENDENCIES: Add for LabRuntime testing:
   [workspace.dev-dependencies]
   asupersync = { path = "/dp/asupersync", features = ["test-internals"] }
   # Provides LabRuntime, oracles, DPOR schedule exploration for deterministic tests","created_at":"2026-02-13T21:46:23Z"},{"id":236,"issue_id":"bd-3un.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"REVISION (review pass 7 - opt-level correction):

The bead body says "opt-level='z'" but AGENTS.md mandates opt-level=3. This is a search library where performance is the primary concern — opt-level=3 (maximum performance) is correct, NOT opt-level='z' (minimum size).

CORRECTED release profile:
  [profile.release]
  opt-level = 3       # Maximum performance optimization (NOT 'z')
  lto = true          # Link-time optimization
  codegen-units = 1   # Single codegen unit for better optimization
  strip = true        # Remove debug symbols

This matches the release profile specified in AGENTS.md exactly.
","created_at":"2026-02-13T21:49:01Z"},{"id":238,"issue_id":"bd-3un.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"CORRECTNESS FIX: opt-level discrepancy

The description says `opt-level='z'` but AGENTS.md correctly specifies `opt-level = 3`.

- opt-level='z': optimizes for binary SIZE (smaller output)
- opt-level=3: optimizes for execution SPEED (maximum performance)

For a search library where sub-millisecond SIMD dot products matter,
opt-level=3 is the correct choice. The description's 'z' is an error.

Correct release profile (per AGENTS.md):
  [profile.release]
  opt-level = 3       # Maximum performance optimization
  lto = true          # Link-time optimization
  codegen-units = 1   # Single codegen unit for better optimization
  strip = true        # Remove debug symbols

Implementers: use opt-level = 3, NOT 'z'.
","created_at":"2026-02-13T21:50:12Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-3un.10","title":"Implement model manifest and SHA256 verification","description":"Implement the model manifest system that tracks required model files, their SHA256 checksums, and HuggingFace repo details. This ensures reproducible, verifiable model installations.

pub struct ModelManifest {
    pub id: String,               // e.g., 'all-minilm-l6-v2'
    pub repo: String,             // HuggingFace repo path
    pub revision: String,         // Pinned commit SHA for reproducibility
    pub files: Vec<ModelFile>,    // Required files with checksums
    pub license: String,          // SPDX identifier
}

pub struct ModelFile {
    pub name: String,             // Path in repo (e.g., 'onnx/model.onnx')
    pub sha256: String,           // Expected SHA256 hex string
    pub size: u64,                // Expected file size in bytes
}

pub enum ModelState {
    NotInstalled,
    NeedsConsent,                 // User must approve before download
    Downloading { progress_pct: u8, bytes_downloaded: u64, total_bytes: u64 },
    Verifying,
    Ready,
    Disabled { reason: String },
    VerificationFailed { reason: String },
    UpdateAvailable { current_revision: String, latest_revision: String },
    Cancelled,
}

Key principles (from cass src/search/model_download.rs):
- NO network calls without explicit user consent (consent-gated downloads)
- Placeholder checksum constant: 'PLACEHOLDER_VERIFY_AFTER_DOWNLOAD'
- Production-ready = has_verified_checksums() && has_pinned_revision()
- Atomic installation: download to temp dir, verify, then rename into place

Built-in manifests:
- ModelManifest::minilm_v2() - the baseline quality model
- ModelManifest::potion_128m() - the fast tier model

Verification:
- SHA256 streaming verification during download (sha2 crate)
- Post-install verification on model load
- Version upgrade detection (compare revisions)","acceptance_criteria":"1. The feature is fully implemented according to this bead design, scope, and referenced files or modules.
2. Integration with dependent components is complete and behavior remains correct across normal and degraded flows.
3. Edge and error cases are explicitly handled with deterministic outcomes and clear reason codes or errors.
4. Unit and integration tests (and e2e where relevant) are added, passing, and include detailed structured logging assertions.
5. Performance and resource expectations in the bead are validated and documented with reproducible evidence.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"SnowyDune","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:49:26.536014967Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-14T01:21:42.757470495Z","closed_at":"2026-02-14T01:21:42.757451359Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["model-mgmt","phase3"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3un.10","depends_on_id":"bd-3un.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:55:12.408296236Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":111,"issue_id":"bd-3un.10","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"REVISION: Model Manifest and Consent Flow

1. Consent-Gated Downloads:
   The manifest system enforces NO network access without explicit user consent.
   State machine: NotInstalled -> NeedsConsent -> [user approves] -> Downloading -> ...

   Consent mechanisms (in priority order):
   a) Programmatic: ModelManifest::set_consent(true) in code
   b) Environment: FRANKENSEARCH_ALLOW_DOWNLOAD=1
   c) Interactive: prompt on stderr if TTY detected
   d) Config file: frankensearch.toml consent = true

   Without consent, auto_detect() returns HashOnly (hash embedder always works).
   This is critical for CI/CD environments where network access is unexpected.

2. Built-in Manifests:
   Two models ship as compile-time constants:
   - MiniLM-L6-v2: sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2
     SHA256 for model.onnx: [computed at implementation time]
     Total size: ~90MB (5 files)
   - potion-128M: minishlab/potion-base-128M
     SHA256 for model.safetensors: [computed at implementation time]
     Total size: ~32MB (2 files)

3. Manifest Extensibility:
   Users can register custom manifests via:
   ModelManifest::register(ModelManifest { id: "my-model", ... });
   This allows custom embedders to participate in the manifest/verification system.

4. Version Pinning:
   Each manifest includes a HuggingFace revision hash.
   Verification fails if the downloaded files don't match the pinned revision.
   This prevents silent model updates from changing search quality.

5. Verification Performance:
   SHA256 of a 90MB file: ~200ms (streaming, not loaded into memory).
   Verification runs at load time, NOT at every search call.
   Cached verification: write .verified sentinel file after first check.
","created_at":"2026-02-13T20:57:47Z"},{"id":231,"issue_id":"bd-3un.10","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"REVIEW FIX — Test requirements for bd-3un.10 (Model Manifest):
- Manifest validation: invalid JSON → clear error
- SHA256 verification: correct hash passes, wrong hash fails, truncated file fails
- State machine: Available → Downloading → Available (success), Downloading → Failed (error)
- progress_pct: values in 0..=100 range
- Placeholder detection: PLACEHOLDER_VERIFY_AFTER_DOWNLOAD is rejected in release builds
- Cancelled → recovery: re-download succeeds after previous cancel
- Empty manifest: no models listed → no crash
- File permissions: model file not readable → clear error","created_at":"2026-02-13T21:47:34Z"}]}
